finding: After a successful application the only confirmation is a small line of green text beside the button: "Thanks. We received your details and will follow up shortly." For a non-native reader this is easy to miss and the phrase "follow up" is a phrasal verb that is ambiguous (will they email me? call me? when?).

evidence.quote: Thanks. We received your details and will follow up shortly.

persona_rationale: I read English carefully and slowly, so a tiny inline message that does not redirect or change the page makes me unsure whether my application actually went through; "follow up shortly" tells me neither the channel nor the timeframe, so I cannot plan.

suggested_fix.current: Thanks. We received your details and will follow up shortly.
suggested_fix.proposed: Redirect to /thank-you-for-applying, or show a large confirmation: "Application received. We will email you within 2 business days."

finding: The nav item, the URL and the page heading use three different words for the same section: nav says "NEWS", the route is /news, but the page heading is "BLOG" with the eyebrow "Insights from the route". A non-native reader cannot tell whether News and Blog are the same place.

evidence.quote: Insights from the route
heading "Blog" [level=1]
BLOG

persona_rationale: When I click "NEWS" and arrive on a page titled "BLOG", I assume I clicked the wrong link or got redirected; in my language News and Blog are different concepts, so the mismatch makes me distrust the navigation.

suggested_fix.current: nav "NEWS" -> page heading "BLOG"
suggested_fix.proposed: Use one word everywhere: heading "News" to match the nav item and /news route.

finding: Intro copy uses an idiom and a non-literal verb: "...the back-office work that makes a vending route compound. New posts drop here as we publish them." "compound" used as a verb and "posts drop" are not literal and do not translate.

evidence.quote: Strategies on locations, machine selection, products, and the back-office work that makes a vending route compound. New posts drop here as we publish them.

persona_rationale: I know "compound" as a noun (a fenced area) and "drop" as the action of falling; using them as slang for "grow" and "are added" forces me to stop and guess the meaning.

suggested_fix.current: ...makes a vending route compound. New posts drop here as we publish them.
suggested_fix.proposed: ...helps a vending route grow steadily. We add new posts here when we publish them.

finding: The about page uses the idiom "the Rat Race" as a section heading: "How I Escaped The Rat Race". This is a culturally specific idiom with no literal meaning.

evidence.quote: How I Escaped The Rat Race

persona_rationale: "Rat Race" translated literally means a competition between rats; I have to already know the American idiom for the daily corporate grind to understand the heading, which is the most prominent text on the section.

suggested_fix.current: How I Escaped The Rat Race
suggested_fix.proposed: How I Left the Traditional 9-to-5 Job

finding: The primary header CTA reads "STEP INSIDE" while the same destination /apply is also reached by buttons labelled "APPLY NOW". "Step inside" is figurative (you cannot literally step into a website) and does not tell me it leads to an application form.

evidence.quote:
link "Step inside":
    - /url: /apply

link "Apply Now":
    - /url: /apply

persona_rationale: Two different labels for the same action confuse me about whether they go to the same place; "Step inside" reads like an invitation to a room, not a button that opens an application form.

suggested_fix.current: STEP INSIDE (header button) -> /apply
suggested_fix.proposed: Use one consistent label for the apply action everywhere, e.g. "Apply Now".

finding: Testimonials are dense with idioms and slang that a second-language reader must decode: "game changer", "hard charging", "learning the biz", "In just a blink of time", "Namaste, indeed, to the journey ahead!". These are the trust-building proof, but the meaning is locked behind colloquialisms.

evidence.quote: Vendingpreneur has been an absolute game changer for us!
so many hard charging, intelligent entrepreneurs
an invaluable resource for learning the biz
In just a blink of time, Mike Hoffman and Vendingpreneurs transformed my journey
Namaste, indeed, to the journey ahead!

persona_rationale: These quotes are the evidence I rely on to trust the program, but I cannot fully understand them; "learning the biz", "hard charging" and "Namaste, indeed" are slang/cultural references that make the proof feel exclusionary to non-Americans.

suggested_fix.current: Verbatim slang-heavy testimonials with no plain-language summary
suggested_fix.proposed: Add a one-line plain-English takeaway under each testimonial (e.g. "Result: 10 locations in 6 months") so meaning is accessible even when the quote uses slang.

finding: Submitting the apply form with empty required fields produces NO visible validation error; the form silently does nothing. A non-native user has no message to read and cannot tell what is wrong.

evidence.quote: empty: no visible validation errors after empty submit
invalid: no visible validation errors on invalid data — entered values preserved

persona_rationale: When nothing appears after I press Submit, I do not know if the site is broken, slow, or rejecting my data; without any error text I have no words to translate and no way to fix the problem.

suggested_fix.current: No validation message on empty/invalid submit
suggested_fix.proposed: Show a specific inline error per field, e.g. "Email is required" / "Enter a valid email address like name@example.com".

finding: The State dropdown lists only US states (Alabama...Wyoming) with no country context, and budget options use the abbreviation "k" ("Under $5k", "$5k-$10k", "$25k+") and currency without a code. A non-American reader may not recognise "$25k+" or know whether "$" means USD.

evidence.quote: Under $5k

persona_rationale: "k" as shorthand for thousand and a bare "$" sign are not universal; I have to pause to confirm "$5k" means five thousand US dollars, and the US-only state list signals the form does not expect someone like me.

suggested_fix.current: Under $5k / $5k-$10k / $25k+ (state list US-only, no country)
suggested_fix.proposed: Spell amounts with currency code, e.g. "Under US$5,000", and add a Country field or label the State field "State (US)".

finding: Contact intro copy uses the noun phrase "accelerator fit" with no explanation: "Send the team a note about vending locations, machine decisions, partnerships, or accelerator fit." "accelerator fit" is jargon a second-language reader cannot parse.

evidence.quote: Send the team a note about vending locations, machine decisions, partnerships, or accelerator fit.

persona_rationale: "accelerator fit" combines a program name with the noun "fit" in a way that has no clear meaning to me; I would not know whether my question belongs in this category.

suggested_fix.current: ...partnerships, or accelerator fit.
suggested_fix.proposed: ...partnerships, or whether the Accelerator program is right for you.

finding: Contact is only reachable from the footer link "CONTACT US"; it is not in the header navigation. A non-native user scanning the prominent top menu for a familiar word will not find a way to ask a question.

evidence.quote:
navigation "Primary":
    - link "About"
    - link "Resources"
    - link "Case Studies"
    - link "News"

persona_rationale: When I am unsure of the English on a page, the first thing I look for is a Contact link in the main menu so I can ask; hiding it in the footer means I may give up before scrolling to the very bottom.

suggested_fix.current: Contact link only in footer
suggested_fix.proposed: Add "Contact" to the header Primary navigation alongside About, Resources, Case Studies, News.

finding: The same content type is named four different ways in the admin: sidebar/route is "News", the editor breadcrumb is "Blog CMS", the heading is "New blog post", and the public nav calls it "NEWS" / public heading "BLOG". "CMS" is also an unexplained abbreviation.

evidence.quote: Blog CMS
heading "New blog post" [level=1]
Create an article or announcement from the shared CMS backend.

persona_rationale: As an admin I must hold four different labels in my head for one thing while also translating; the abbreviation "CMS" and "backend" are technical English with no plain-language fallback.

suggested_fix.current: Blog CMS / New blog post / News (route) / shared CMS backend
suggested_fix.proposed: Pick one term ("News") and use it consistently; replace "shared CMS backend" with "Create a news article or announcement."

finding: Workflow filter labels are terse jargon with no tooltips: "Needs review", "Updating", "Needs links", "Metadata issues", "Scheduled", "Schedule failed". "SEO readiness" states are likewise abbreviated to single words "Blocked", "Opportunities", "Needs work".

evidence.quote: Needs review
Metadata issues
SEO readiness: Opportunities
SEO readiness: Needs work

persona_rationale: "Opportunities" as a status does not tell me if it is good or bad, and "Needs links" is too compressed to parse without context; for a second-language admin these one-word states are guesswork.

suggested_fix.current: Opportunities / Needs links / Metadata issues
suggested_fix.proposed: Use full descriptive labels with a tooltip, e.g. "Some SEO improvements available", "Missing internal links", "Page metadata needs fixing".

finding: The page description mixes jargon: "Manage structured SEO pages with the same CMS shell ready for resource content." "CMS shell" and "structured SEO pages" are unexplained technical noun phrases.

evidence.quote: Manage structured SEO pages with the same CMS shell ready for resource content.

persona_rationale: "CMS shell" is opaque even to many native speakers; for me it is two abstract nouns I cannot map to anything on screen, so the description does not help me understand the page.

suggested_fix.current: Manage structured SEO pages with the same CMS shell ready for resource content.
suggested_fix.proposed: Create and edit SEO and resource pages for the website.

finding: Headline stat counters display literal placeholder zeros: "0+" Entrepreneurs launched, "$0M+" Snack/Drink sales, "0+" Vending locations. A reader who takes text literally sees the proof numbers as zero.

evidence.quote:
term: 0+
  - definition: Entrepreneurs launched

term: $0M+
  - definition: Snack/Drink sales in Vendingpreneur machines

persona_rationale: I read numbers literally and do not assume they are animated counters that will rise; "0+ entrepreneurs launched" reads to me as "nobody has launched", which undermines trust.

suggested_fix.current: 0+ / $0M+ / 0+ (animated-from-zero counters)
suggested_fix.proposed: Render the real final figures server-side so the static text shows actual numbers (e.g. "500+"), not "0+".

finding: A legacy /blog/... article URL returns a 404. Because the public page heading is "BLOG", a non-native user who guesses the URL from the heading word lands on a dead page.

evidence.quote: 404 /blog/how-to-choose-the-perfect-location-for-vending-machine

persona_rationale: I learned the section is called "Blog", so typing /blog/... feels logical; getting a 404 makes me think the article was deleted rather than that the path simply moved.

suggested_fix.current: /blog/{slug} returns 404
suggested_fix.proposed: Add a 301 redirect from /blog/{slug} to /news/{slug} so the word users see ("Blog") maps to a working URL.

finding: On first paint the article cards render as blank outlines until scrolled into view, and a cover image fails with a 500 error (broken-image alt text is shown). A non-native user sees an empty/broken page and no message explaining it.

evidence.quote: broken image showing alt text 'Top 10 Profitable Products to Stock in Your Vending Machine...'
500 /_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcdn.prod.website-files.com...

persona_rationale: A broken image plus blank cards reads to me as a loading failure or an error in my own browser; with no English message to confirm the content is just loading, I assume the site is broken and may leave.

suggested_fix.current: Blank scroll-reveal cards + failing cover image (500) on load
suggested_fix.proposed: Render card content on first paint (reveal only the animation, not the content) and fix or replace the broken cover image so no broken-image placeholder shows.

finding: Body copy uses the idiom "increase your bottom line" and the metaphor "eliminate the guesswork". "bottom line" is a financial idiom that does not translate literally.

evidence.quote: bulk product deals to increase your bottom line.
We eliminate the guesswork by providing a structured roadmap to success.

persona_rationale: "bottom line" literally means the last line of a page; I must already know it is American business slang for profit, which adds friction to otherwise clear marketing copy.

suggested_fix.current: increase your bottom line
suggested_fix.proposed: increase your profit

finding: Article preview compresses meaning into ambiguous shorthand: "success track record (300% boost via vetted options)". "vetted options" and "300% boost" with no baseline are unclear without context.

evidence.quote: success track record (300% boost via vetted options), ongoing tools. L...

persona_rationale: "vetted" is an uncommon word for me and "300% boost" gives a percentage with no baseline, so I cannot tell what improved by 300% or compared to what.

suggested_fix.current: 300% boost via vetted options
suggested_fix.proposed: up to 300% higher sales using pre-checked machine and location choices

finding: The thank-you page exists with clear copy ("Watch your inbox for the next step.") but the apply form never routes to it, so non-native users get the unclear inline message instead of this readable page.

evidence.quote: Watch your inbox for the next step.
Thanks. We received your details and will follow up shortly.

persona_rationale: This page would answer all my questions in plain steps ("Watch your inbox", "Have your goals ready"), but I never see it because the form does not navigate here; the clearer copy is wasted.

suggested_fix.current: Apply form shows inline text and stays on /apply
suggested_fix.proposed: Redirect a successful apply submission to /thank-you-for-applying, which already has clearer next-step copy.

finding: Open-text field label "What are you trying to build?" is vague for a second-language reader and overlaps with the intro line "...what kind of support would move the business forward." It is unclear what answer is expected.

evidence.quote: What are you trying to build?
Share where you are now, where you want to launch, and what kind of support would move the business forward.

persona_rationale: "What are you trying to build?" is abstract; I am not sure if it means a vending business, a website, or something else, and there is no example to guide a non-native writer.

suggested_fix.current: What are you trying to build?
suggested_fix.proposed: Tell us about your vending goals (optional) — e.g. "I want 5 machines in offices within a year."
